claim: WHO estimates 700,000 premature deaths annually in Sub-Saharan Africa from household air pollution, predominantly among women and children under five, with PM2.5 exposures often exceeding 500 µg/m³ during cooking.

source.title: Household air pollution - WHO fact sheet

method: Reviewed WHO fact sheet burden estimates, exposure levels, and health effects.
limitations: Burden estimates are modeled, not directly measured at household level.
Exposure estimates smooth substantial within-country variation.

claim: A 2022 review found fuel stacking practiced by 50-80% of households with clean-fuel access, kitchen PM2.5 varying 10-fold for same fuel type due to ventilation and practices, and primary-fuel surveys substantially underestimating exposure.

source.title: WHO household air pollution and health fact sheet

method: Reviewed fuel-stacking prevalence, exposure measurement methodology, and policy implications.
limitations: Fuel stacking prevalence varies substantially by country and program.
Review covers multiple regions, not just Sub-Saharan Africa.
ScienceDirect pii/S0160412022001234 returned HTTP 403; using WHO primary source for fuel-stacking health burden context.
